sentences.text: My name is Moshe Taube.
I was known in Poland as Maksymilian Taube.
They called me either Moshe or Monusch.
T-A-U-B-E is my last name.
Moshe, M-O-S-H-E.
Or, Maksymilian, M-A-K-S-Y-M-I-L-I-A-N.
This was my name at birth, yes.
No.
Not that I'm aware of.
My Hebrew name is Moshe [HEBREW].
Which means, he was drawn out from water.
And in my life I was really in much of deep water.
No.
I was born June 17, 1927.
I am, today, 69.
I was born in Cracow Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was the cradle of the Jewish culture, Jewish learning, and Jewish scholarship.
My father's name was Emanuel, E-M-A-N-U-E-L.
And my mother's name was Rachel, Hebrew, or Regina, R-E-G-I-N-A.
She was usually called Regina.
Rachel she was called only when [LAUGHS] somebody wanted to make her feel good, like my grandmother or some sisters of hers.
My father was an industrious man, a man of business.
He had a very great knack of-- for travel, for-- for-- for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] entrepreneurship, as we say it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother received a dowry from her family, and the dowry was a house, which later was sold and converted into a-- beginning of a shirt factory.
My father was, of course, the head of the enterprise.
And we had also a partner, whose name was Avrum Wrobel.
Wrobel.
Yes, he was a-- he was [HEBREW].
And since my father came from a dynasty of [HEBREW], so the partnership was only natural between him and Wrobel.
W-R-O- with the [INAUDIBLE] B-E-L.
Unfortunately, he and his family were all exterminated during the Holocaust.
The business was named as a shirt factory, [FRENCH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is like a-- like a French name.
The factory-- the shirts that my father manufactured were known under the name of [FRENCH].
Which means, special shirts--
--in French.
No.
No.
No.
As a matter of fact, only my mother [INAUDIBLE] helped out in the business, and she was there almost daily, to oversee the work and to-- also to be a part of a very growing and thriving business.
It was really thriving, because my father started very small-- on a very small scale-- and it developed into a very serious-- very serious manufacturing business.
He was home, of course.
But he traveled a lot, because to sell shirts, you see, to make contact with-- with clienteleship, with clientele, she had-- he had to travel in Poland-- mostly in Poland.
But most of the time, he went in the morning, and he was back at night.
Yes.
My father, who was a traditional man, he had a very fine and pleasant voice.
And he'd really like to be a [HEBREW].
He very-- he was really tickled when-- when asked to daven a [HEBREW] Shabbat or on a Shabbat when he was in shul.
Usually, we went-- my father liked a small shul where there was a rabbi.
Of course, the rabbi was connected with the [HEBREW]-- with the [HEBREW].
And he-- he liked to-- a small-- a small surrounding, a small room where they davened, I would say-- unofficially.
We lived in Cracow.
And I was born in Cracow on Dietla Street, D-I-E-T-L-A-- Dietla Street.
And as a matter of fact, we moved on Dietla Street three times-- or, really, twice-- from 29-- number 29, where I was born-- to 57.
It was a few years, and then 63 where the war-- when the war came and found us, right there at Dietla 63.
My mother was a very devoted-- devoted [HEBREW].
She was a devoted homemaker.
In spite of having the chores of helping out in the factory, she was a wonderful homemaker.
She was a wonderful cook.
She was [HEBREW]-- with heart and soul-- tied and devoted to the children, and of course to my father.
And I remember Shabbat was a beautiful occasion of togetherness, of spiritual fulfillment, and, of course, of good food.
I remember the gefilte fish my mother-- my mother used to make.
I remember the beautiful meats-- the variety of meats-- that she prepared for Shabbat.
Not only for Shabbat, for everyday.
She cooked every day.
And in spite of us having a live-in maid, she was always there in the afternoon to cook, for us, dinner and to be-- to be of help, not only in the factory, but to be of help in the-- for the family.
Every Shabbat.
There was no Shabbat without Cholent.
It was-- it was-- it was a integral part of a Shabbat celebration.
Cholent?
Of course.
Oh, of course, the regular-- beans, meat, sometimes a hard-boiled egg.
It was delicious.
But there were special recipes that my mother had that nobody can match.
Perhaps my wife some-- can match it, yes.
And not only the Cholent, but beautiful cakes that she'd bake.
And not only cakes, the challahs.
She baked challahs for every Shabbat.
And you didn't have such facilities like we have today.
It was an oven, you know, a-- an oven which was-- which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on coals.
It was heated by coals, an oven with coals.
You know, the old-fashioned ovens, the-- the-- you know.
And-- and it took quite a-- an effort to bake challahs, to bake cakes, and to cook.
Quite an effort.
My religious upbringing was really traditional, I would say.
From the age of five, I was every day in the Heder with two rabbis teaching me-- first [HEBREW], then [HEBREW], and then to write in Hebrew.
You see?
It was-- it was an experience that I will never forget.

Because those rabbis, in spite of the fact that their compensation was really minimal, really minimal, they gave their everything, their all, to impart their knowledge, and to instill the fear of God into the-- to the children who participated in the Heder.
Yes.
I remember one-- one of my rabbis.
His name was Isaac.
I know because everybody was afraid of him, because the discipline was just very stringent with him.
You have to be very, very well-behaved, and you had to know your stuff.
Otherwise, there was a little strap that he had, and wow, that little strap hurt sometimes.
Sometimes.
I wouldn't say-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not often, not often.
I lost track of them.
I really-- I don't remember their names, and I don't remember whether they are alive.
I-- nobody came forward.
See, I know that there were kids who-- there were about 15 kids in a Heder, you see?
But I lost track of them.
I lost track of them.
There is only one kid, who went with me to the-- to school, that, all of a sudden, when he read about me, he remembered me.
He's now in-- in England-- in Essex, in England, next to London.
And all of a sudden, I get a beautiful letter from him that I'm your long-lost classmate, the only classmate that came forward.
And he sent me that little-- the picture where my-- my class is featured, and myself being six years old.
I was really astounded to hear from him.
He remembered me.
I would never remember him, because, by his name or by his looks.
But he remembered me, because he said I was somebody special there-- my face or my smile.
Who knows?
What's his name?
His name is Moshe Richter.
Richter.
He's my age.
He lives in England.
Unfortunately, he has some sorrows, some trouble with his son-- not a very happy man.
About, I would say, eight months ago.
Eight months ago.
Because he happened to read the book, where-- where, you know, the book is now available all over the world.
And he happened to come across this book, and Moshe Taube?
Oh, of course.
Of course, of course.
That's my classmate.
And he immediately contacted me, and told me who he is, where we studied, how we studied.
And-- and he brought to my mind so-- so many things that I forgot already.
I completely forgot.
Like our trip to salt mines of Wielczka, next to Cracow.
I forgot all about it.
And not only this, but he sent me the picture from the salt mines of Wielczka, the class picture that-- from 1936.
[INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It just came back to me.
It came back to me, yes.
It's the childhood-- the childhood.
Yes.
I had a three years young-- a sister that was three years younger than myself.
Her name was Nina, N-I-N-A.
[SIGHS] She and my mother [INAUDIBLE] were-- were all killed by the Germans in [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
And-- she of course--
About her?
We were always fighting, like everywhere, you know, in every family.
She wanted this, and I wanted that.
And, you know, why she and not me-- and all the little, little insignificant fights, really, which-- child stuff.
And-- but we were a very close-knit family.
A very close-knit family.
And she went to school, herself.
And she was very musical-- very musical, like my mother and my father.
They were all fond of music, of songs, of-- my goodness-- of opera.
My mother, my father were just op-- great opera buffs.
And the house reverberated sounds of opera all the time.
But besides opera, we had folk songs.
Come to my mind, a little folk song that I-- I learned as a little boy of six or seven.
[HEBREW].
This is the famous pronouncement of [HEBREW], the healer of the elderly in [HEBREW], in the 'Ethics of the Father.' It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] set to a song, this [HEBREW].
This is famous pronounces. 'If I am not for myself, who is for me?
But if I am for myself, who am I?
And, if not now, when?' So the song went like this.
[SINGING IN HEBREW]
This was a song that was sung very popularly in Cracow.
In Cracow.
And this came to my mind.
As a little child I learned this little song, which has so much meaning-- which has so much meaning, such deep meaning.
I don't remember.
But if he would, perhaps I was too young to understand this deep meaning.
All I understood is the simple melody of this song, and it stuck with me.

As a matter of fact, when I am sometimes officiating in a mourner's house, when I am conducting the service, between [HEBREW] and [HEBREW], I sometimes deliver the [HEBREW], and it comes out that I quote this saying of [HEBREW].
And I always-- I-- I try to inject that little song, so that people should understand and perhaps remember it better.
Yes.
Always.
Always.
Since I was five, six years old, music was always a part of my life.
Always.
And not only of my life, but the life of family.
Music was a-- an integral feature of everyday life-- of everyday life.
There was not a day without music, either from the radio, or from the piano, or from singing, or from discussing music.
Not a day went by without it.
I started to play the piano-- to study piano, really-- with a teacher at the age of eight.
This is also when I started to sing in a choir with a cant-- with a famous cantor of Cracow, Yossel Mandelbaum, [INAUDIBLE].
He just died three years ago, at the age of 93, 94, in Brooklyn, New York.
He was the cantor in the Cypress Shul in Cracow.
Cypress, C-I-P-R-E-S-S.
C-Y-P-R-E-S-S.
Cypress Shul on [INAUDIBLE] Street in Cracow.
It was a very beautiful synagogue, and he was the only one who had a [HEBREW] and a [HEBREW], because he was a [HEBREW].
Yossel Mandelbaum [INAUDIBLE].
But I will never forget his davening, his fervor, his tremendous impact on the people.
When he was davening-- they-- for example, when he said [HEBREW], when he blessed the new moon, the synagogue was rocking with sobs from-- especially from the women's gallery.
I remember it so distinctly.
There were such tremendous sobs, loud sobs-- from-- from the women's gallery and from people.
Because he moved the people so tremendously.
And he gave me the-- the path.
He showed me the path in [HEBREW], in the [HEBREW].
It means the emotional cantorate-- cantorate-- the emotional cantorial art.
See, because there's-- there are two cantorial arts.
There's the emotional cantorial art, practiced by cantors most in Eastern Europe.
And the straight, the classical, cantorial art practiced in Germany, France, and England, in Western Europe, you see.
So he, by-- with his fervor, with his emot-- his tremendous emotions, showed me the way to the [HEBREW], the emotional cantorial art.
Did you have anyone else living in the house with you?
No.
This was only-- no.
Yes, of course.
There was the maid, a live-in maid.
We had always a live-in maid to help out in cleaning and going with the children sometimes to-- for a walk, and, you know, when my mother was busy in the factory, and so on.
Her name was Zosia.
Zosia, Z-O-S-I-A.
No.
No.
As a matter of fact, I had a nurse who nursed me.
She was not Jewish either.
Her name was [?
Antosha. ?] But she loved me like-- like I would be her son.
I remember it distinctly.
There was, in Poland, in Cracow, the school on Dietla Street.
It is-- it was called Baraki, B-A-R-A-K-I.
Baraki.
Why Baraki?
Because it was like a barracks, like a big long barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A wooden-- a wooden structure in the middle of the street.
It was like a-- like a-- like an island in the street.
You see?
It was a big street.
An island in the street, and in the middle of the island was the school.
There I went for six grades.
Yes.
And the director of the school was Horinger.
His name was Horinger, H-O-R-I-N-G-E-R.
Horinger.
What type of a school was it?
This was a Jewish school, meaning there was-- there were only Jewish students in the school.
But it was a secular school.
We-- we did learn [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some religion in the school but not-- but it was not the-- it was only one feature, one-- I would say one period in two days or three days of religion.
Mostly what we did was secular subjects.
But after the school, I went to the Heder, of course, to-- to learn in depth [HEBREW], [HEBREW], and Jewish lore, and the customs and ceremonies, and [HEBREW] and laws.
This was a public school.
You mean the-- you mean the Baraki school?
It was a public school.
Yes.
It was a state school, really.
But there were only Jews in the school.
The teachers were mostly Jewish.
Some were not.
Some were not.
I remember--
I remember my first grade teacher.
He was not Jewish, but he was adorable.
His name was Niemczyk.
Pan Niemczyk.
Mr.
Niemczyk.
N-I-E-M-C-Z-Y-K.
Niemczyk.
I remember it like today.
And we loved him, because he was such a gentleman.
He was so wonderful with the children that the memory is just eternal.
And, of course, I had also a one music teacher who was not Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He liked my singing very much as a child.
And he used to say to me, you might be a singer.
He used to say to me.
This also I remember.
You see, it's also a memory.
You might be a singer.
Because I had a very good voice, a strong voice, as a child.
I just stared, because I didn't know what a singer was.
[LAUGHS] At five, six years old.
I knew that singers were in the opera and-- and on the concert stage.
But, me, a singer?
Where?
What?
Yes.
When I was nine years old, I joined a-- an organization in Cracow, a Zionist organization.
It was called Akiba, A-K-I-B-A.
Yes, Akiba.
This was a secular organization.
It was not a-- not left and not right.
It was a center-mind-- center-oriented org-- Zionist organization.
And there I was exposed to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] atmosphere, the knowledge, and the history of Zionism.
No, but there were-- there were-- listen, there were very few non-religious Jews in Cracow.
It was definitely a minority of non-religious Jews.
So whoever belonged there must have been at least traditional.
Oh, they encouraged it.
They encouraged it.
It was-- it was-- for them, it was educational.
Educational, not political.
Educational.
They wanted me to be exposed to the aspects of Zionist life and of Eretz Yisrael life.
To Eretz Yisrael?
There was no role model in Cracow of going to Eretz Yisrael.
You see, there was nobody who urged someone to go or who served as a role model by going himself or herself to Israel-- to Eretz Yisrael, at that time.
So it was a-- like a-- a dream, an aspiration, perhaps, a feature of life, but not something practical to-- for one to work or to strive to be-- to emigrate and go to Eretz Yisrael.
This-- this was not on the agenda at that time.
